marketOverview: The Third Gulf War enters its third week with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, Brent crude back above $100/bbl, and a 'Super Week' of seven major central bank decisions (RBA, BOC, FOMC, ECB, BOE, SNB, BOJ) dominating the calendar. The macro divide is stark: USD and CAD benefit from energy independence, while EUR, GBP, JPY, and CHF face an energy-import tax. The key shift from last week is the RBA — 80% market probability of a hike on Tuesday — which elevates AUD from Neutral to Weak Bullish. NZD remains the most exposed high-beta currency with no RBNZ meeting and no domestic catalyst. The FOMC (Wed) is expected to hold at 3.50-3.75% while the ECB, BOE, and SNB all hold — but the tone of each statement will be critical given the stagflation dilemma facing energy-importing central banks.

currencies.name: US Dollar
currencies.rationale: USD finished last week as the dominant G10 currency, with DXY briefly breaching 100.00 (peak 100.29) as safe-haven demand, energy independence, and a hawkish front-end repricing converged. Core PCE printed 3.1% YoY (above 3.0% exp), Jobless Claims held at 213k, and money markets now price just 18bps of Fed easing for the year. The FOMC on Wednesday is expected to hold at 3.50-3.75% with PPI the same day — if both confirm the stagflation narrative, the Dollar can extend gains. The near-term bullish call softens toward neutral only if the FOMC sounds more dovish than expected or if the geopolitical premium fades materially.
drivers: Safe-haven and liquidity premium: DXY peaked at 100.29, YTD highs
Core PCE 3.1% YoY (above 3.0% exp) — hot inflation keeps Fed on hold
Money markets: only 18bps of Fed easing priced for full year 2026
Energy exporter advantage: US terms-of-trade benefit vs EUR/JPY
Jobless Claims 213k — labor market stable despite Q4 GDP revised to 0.7%
FOMC (WED) + PPI (WED) + Unemployment Claims (THU) key catalysts

currencies.name: Euro
currencies.rationale: EUR endured a grueling week as Europe's energy import dependency became the dominant market narrative — Brent above $100/bbl acts as a direct tax on the Eurozone economy, and Sentix Investor Confidence plunged to -3.1 (from 4.2) in the first hard data point reflecting the Iran war shock. Money markets swung from pricing ECB cuts to nearly 45bps of hikes by year-end, but EUR could not hold the rates support as the market treats higher energy costs as a growth problem first. The ECB meets Thursday with updated macro projections — the tone on whether to 'look through' the supply shock or lean against it will be the key EUR catalyst of the week.
drivers: Energy importer shock: Brent $100+ acts as direct tax on Eurozone economy
Sentix Investor Confidence -3.1 (from 4.2) — first hard data of Iran war impact
ECB repricing: markets now price 45bps of hikes by year-end (was cuts)
German Factory Orders -11.1% in January, EU Industrial Production -1.5% MoM
EUR/USD fragile near 1.1200 target; EUR/CHF relatively stable (both energy-short)
ECB Meeting (THU) + updated macro projections are the key catalyst

currencies.name: British Pound
currencies.rationale: Sterling succumbed to the stagflation narrative last week as flat January GDP (0.0% vs 0.2% exp) combined with the energy shock to leave the BoE in a policy straitjacket. While gilt yields spiked (10-year to 4.78%) and BoE rate cuts were fully priced out, the Pound could not hold the rates support — a clear signal the market is treating higher yields as a symptom of policy stress rather than a currency positive. Money markets now price 19bps of tightening by year-end. The BoE meets Thursday; the vote split and Bailey's tone on growth vs inflation will determine whether GBP can recover or continues to lag.
drivers: Stagflation trap: GDP 0.0% MoM (miss) + energy-driven inflation surge
BoE rate cuts fully priced out; 19bps of hikes now priced by year-end
10-year Gilt yield peaked at 4.78% — but Pound could not hold rates support
BoE/Ipsos inflation expectations 3.2% (pre-war, likely to rise further)
Political noise: Mandelson documents, Reeves G7 focus on energy security
UK Jobs Report (WED) + BOE Meeting (THU) are the key catalysts

currencies.name: Japanese Yen
currencies.rationale: JPY suffered a haven failure last week as Japan's extreme energy import dependence (90%+ of crude from the Middle East) overwhelmed the usual defensive bid, pushing USD/JPY deep into the 158-160 intervention zone. The market is treating Japan as one of the clearest losers from sustained energy disruption — Reuters noted the bar for FX intervention has risen because the Yen's weakness is fundamentally driven by oil and Dollar strength rather than speculative excess. Finance Minister Katayama warned of 'all necessary steps' but was largely ignored. The BOJ meets Thursday and is widely expected to hold at 0.75% — any dovish signal would accelerate the move toward 160.00.
drivers: Haven failure: energy importer shock overwhelmed defensive bid
Japan imports 90%+ of crude from Middle East — direct terms-of-trade hit
USD/JPY in 158-160 intervention zone; Reuters: bar for intervention has risen
Q4 GDP revised slightly higher but Household Spending contracted
BOJ expected to hold at 0.75% — no near-term normalization signal
BOJ Meeting (THU) is the key catalyst; 160.00 is the line in the sand

currencies.name: Australian Dollar
currencies.rationale: AUD was elevated from Neutral to Weak Bullish this week by a decisive hawkish shift in RBA expectations. RBA Deputy Governor Hauser flagged a 'very genuine policy debate' at the March 17 meeting, triggering sell-side revisions from Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, Westpac, and NAB — all now forecasting a hike to 4.10%. Money markets assign 80% probability to a 25bps hike on Tuesday. Australia's energy exporter status and firmer China trade data (Jan-Feb beats across Trade Balance, Imports, and Exports) provide additional support. The key risk is a late-week reversal if the RBA does not validate the hawkish shift or if global risk sentiment deteriorates again.
drivers: RBA hike 80% probability: Goldman, BofA, Westpac, NAB all forecast 4.10%
RBA Deputy Governor Hauser: 'very genuine policy debate' at March 17 meeting
Australia energy exporter: benefits from higher oil prices (unlike EUR/JPY)
China Jan-Feb Trade: Exports, Imports, Trade Balance all beat expectations
LME Copper tested $13,000/t but late-week metals slide capped AUD gains
RBA Meeting (TUE) + Employment Report (THU) are the key catalysts

currencies.name: Canadian Dollar
currencies.rationale: CAD endured extreme two-way volatility last week and ultimately underperformed despite WTI surging toward $120/bbl — a shock -83.9k employment print (vs +10k exp) and a widening trade deficit showed the Canadian economy was already weakening before the full war impact. USD/CAD surged from 1.3654 to 1.3715 on Friday, decisively reclaiming the 50-day MA at 1.3700. The commodity channel still offers support and the BoC faces an inflation constraint from triple-digit oil, but this week's CPI (Monday) and BOC Meeting (Wednesday) will determine whether the energy windfall or the labor market deterioration wins the narrative. Downgraded from Bullish to Weak Bullish after Friday's data shock.
drivers: Employment shock: -83.9k (vs +10k exp), unemployment 6.7% — major miss
Trade deficit widened to C$3.65B (vs C$0.9B exp); exports -4.7%
WTI still above $100/bbl — commodity channel provides floor for CAD
BoC: markets price 43bps of hikes by year-end (inflation constraint from oil)
USD/CAD reclaimed 50-day MA at 1.3700 after jobs shock
CPI (MON) + BOC Meeting (WED) + Retail Sales (FRI) are key catalysts

currencies.name: Swiss Franc
currencies.rationale: CHF struggled to assert its traditional safe-haven status last week as the energy shock neutralized the Franc's usual crisis appeal — Switzerland is a net energy importer, and the aggressive hawkish repricing of the Fed widened the yield disadvantage for the lower-yielding Franc. USD/CHF edged higher while EUR/CHF remained relatively stable, with both currencies treated as energy-short assets. Money markets now price 23bps of SNB hikes by year-end, a sharp reversal from prior easing expectations. The SNB meets Thursday — Chairman Jordan's tone on imported inflation vs currency strength will be the key signal. Neutral is appropriate given the offsetting forces of haven demand and energy vulnerability.
drivers: Energy importer: Franc's haven appeal offset by oil shock (like EUR)
SNB repricing: 23bps of hikes priced by year-end (was cuts)
EUR/CHF stable: both currencies treated as energy-short assets
Weekly sight deposits fell to CHF 454B — no aggressive SNB intervention
Swiss Consumer Confidence -30 (slightly weaker than -29 exp)
SNB Meeting (THU) — Jordan's tone on inflation vs currency strength is key

currencies.name: New Zealand Dollar
currencies.rationale: NZD remains the most exposed G10 currency to the current environment — a high-beta risk proxy with no domestic hawkish catalyst (unlike AUD's RBA hike story), full dependence on global sentiment, and no RBNZ meeting until April. The Kiwi outperformed briefly during the mid-week Trump de-escalation rally but gave back all gains as risk-off returned. China data provided a floor (Jan-Feb trade beats) but not a bullish catalyst. With NZD GDP on Wednesday as the only domestic release and the RBNZ pricing only ~60bps of hikes by year-end (vs AUD's more aggressive repricing), the Kiwi remains vulnerable to any deterioration in global risk appetite.
drivers: High-beta risk proxy: no domestic hawkish catalyst unlike AUD's RBA story
No RBNZ meeting until April — policy support absent this week
RBNZ pricing: ~60bps of hikes by year-end (less aggressive than AUD repricing)
China trade beats helped floor NZD but not a strong bullish catalyst
AUD/NZD volatile: fell below 1.1900 early week, recovered on RBA hike bets
NZD GDP (WED) is the only domestic release — key risk event

long.rationale: Weak Bullish AUD (RBA hike 80% probability, energy exporter) vs Bearish JPY (energy importer, haven failure, BOJ hold)
Weak Bullish AUD (RBA hike catalyst, domestic rates support) vs Bearish NZD (no RBNZ meeting, high-beta, no catalyst)
Weak Bullish USD (safe-haven, energy exporter, FOMC hold) vs Bearish JPY (energy importer, 158-160 zone)
Weak Bullish CAD (oil windfall, BoC inflation constraint) vs Bearish JPY (energy importer, haven failure)
Weak Bullish AUD (RBA hike, domestic rates support) vs Neutral CHF (energy importer, SNB cap)

short.rationale: Weak Bearish EUR (energy importer, ECB stagflation trap) vs Weak Bullish USD (safe-haven, FOMC hold)
Weak Bearish EUR (energy importer, ECB trapped) vs Weak Bullish AUD (RBA hike 80% probability)
Weak Bearish EUR (energy shock, ECB dilemma) vs Weak Bullish CAD (oil windfall, BoC hawkish)
Weak Bearish GBP (stagflation trap, GDP miss, BoE policy straitjacket) vs Weak Bullish AUD (RBA hike)
Bearish NZD (high-beta, no catalyst, no RBNZ) vs Weak Bullish USD (safe-haven, energy exporter)
